Algeria.text: Algeria's group stage finale against Austria was a breathless, lurching affair: Mahrez equalised in the dying seconds of normal time only for Kalajdzic to restore Austria's lead three minutes into stoppage time, and Algeria scraped through as one of the better third-placed sides rather than on their own terms. Switzerland await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT — a stern, organised European side that will fancy their chances against a Algerian defence that shipped seven goals across the group stage, making this a genuine test rather than a comfortable passage. Survive that, and Argentina or Egypt could loom in the quarterfinal.

Argentina.text: Argentina closed out Group J in their customary manner — two goals before half-time against Jordan, a moment of Messi class in the 80th minute to seal the 3-1 verdict, and a consolation conceded that did nothing to disturb the broader authority of the performance. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture the world champions enter as heavy favorites; the Islanders will make it competitive, but Argentina's quality across the pitch should prove decisive. Survive that, and the draw opens toward the quarterfinal tier, where Colombia or Switzerland could provide a sterner examination of whether this side's group-stage assurance holds under genuine pressure.

Australia.text: Australia ground out a goalless draw with Paraguay to close the group stage — functional rather than fluent, but enough to secure second place in Group D. Egypt await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a genuinely open contest between two sides of comparable standing; neither carries the weight of obvious favouritism, and the Socceroos' compact defensive shape may suit a tight knockout encounter. Survive that, and the prospect of Argentina looms in the Round of 16 — a far sterner examination of just how far this side can go.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J in the most nerve-shredding fashion imaginable: a 3-3 draw with Algeria that swung violently in both directions, Kalajdzic's equaliser arriving in the sixth minute of stoppage time after Mahrez had twice pulled Algeria level and then handed them the lead in the dying seconds. That dramatic finish secured qualification, though it will have done little to settle Austrian nerves ahead of a Round of 32 appointment with Spain on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT — a formidable assignment by any measure, with La Roja firmly the favourites and Austria needing a performance well beyond anything they produced in the group. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal await in the Round of 16, ensuring there is no soft landing anywhere in this bracket.

Belgium.text: Belgium survived an almighty scare against Senegal, trailing 2-0 after the hour before Lukaku pulled one back in the 86th minute, Tielemans equalised three minutes later, and the same man settled it from the spot deep into extra time — as dramatic a Round of 32 exit from crisis as you will see. The Round of 16 on Mon Jul 6 17:00 PT brings either Bosnia & Herzegovina or the host nation USA, and while Belgium are clear favourites against either, a raucous American crowd makes the latter no routine assignment. Should they advance, the quarterfinal draw could serve up Portugal or Spain — a genuine examination of whether this Belgium generation can finally convert promise into tournament substance.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with purpose, leading Qatar 2-0 inside 35 minutes before riding out a brief scare when Al-Haydos pulled one back just before half-time, then Mahmic's late third settled the nerves and confirmed their passage as one of the best third-placed sides. The reward is a Round of 32 meeting with the United States on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT — a stern examination against a host nation carrying considerable expectation and the backing of partisan crowds, though Bosnia have shown enough attacking conviction to make it a contest rather than a foregone conclusion. Survive that, and Belgium await in the Round of 16, with Portugal or Spain potentially looming in the quarterfinals as the ultimate measure of how far this team can genuinely reach.

Brazil.text: Brazil edged past Japan in a Round of 32 tie that was far from straightforward — trailing to Sano's opener before Casemiro levelled midway through the second half, the Seleção needed a Martinelli strike deep into stoppage time to see it through. Norway await in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, a fixture Brazil enter as firm favourites but one the Norwegians, physical and well-organised, will not make comfortable. Survive that, and England or Mexico could stand in the way of a semifinal place, with the likes of France, Spain, and Argentina lurking as potential final-four obstacles.

Canada.text: Stephen Eustáquio's stoppage-time winner against South Africa was the narrowest of escapes — a 1-0 result that flatters Canada's composure but confirms their ability to find something when it matters most. Morocco await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT, and this is a genuine test: the Moroccans carry the weight of a nation's ambition and have the defensive organisation to suffocate hosts and heavyweights alike, making this anything but routine for a Canadian side still finding its rhythm. Survive that, and France or Paraguay could stand in the quarterfinal way, with the possibility of Spain or Portugal lurking in the semifinal — the draw offering no mercy the deeper Canada advances.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out a goalless draw against Saudi Arabia to close the group stage — three draws from three matches, never beaten, never quite able to land the punch. That record of quiet resilience earns them a Round of 32 appointment with Argentina on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture that, on any reasonable assessment, represents a mountain: the world champions are among the tournament favourites, and Cape Verde arrive as heavy underdogs. Should the extraordinary occur, Australia or Egypt would await in the Round of 16 — and that, realistically, is where the dream would have to begin in earnest.

Colombia.text: Colombia finished the group stage with a composed 0-0 draw against Portugal — a disciplined, goalless stalemate that nevertheless secured top spot in Group K and underlined their defensive solidity against genuine European quality. They meet Ghana in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a fixture Colombia enter as clear favourites: the Black Stars are capable of disrupting any opponent on their day, but Colombia's form and pedigree make this a winnable assignment without undue alarm. Survive that, and a quarterfinal could bring Argentina into view — a South American derby with continental bragging rights and no shortage of edge.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana in a nervy group-stage finale, Luka Sucic's first-half opener holding up until Derrick Luckassen levelled with twelve minutes left before Nikola Vlasic settled matters in the 83rd — a hard-fought win that reflects a side still finding its rhythm. They now face Portugal in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a genuine test against one of the tournament's heavyweight contenders; Croatia arrive as underdogs, though their resilience makes them dangerous opponents for anyone. Survive that and Spain or Austria could await in the Round of 16, with Belgium or the United States potentially looming in the quarterfinals.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao's group stage ended with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé doing the damage with goals either side of half-time — the first arriving inside seven minutes to set a tone the Curaçao defence never recovered from. They finished fourth in Group E with a single point and a goal difference of minus nine, yet still advanced to the Round of 32 as one of the tournament's best third-placed sides. There, their journey came to an abrupt end, the gulf in quality across the group stage ultimately a true reflection of where this Curaçao side stands on the world stage.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage ended with a thumping 3-0 defeat to Mexico, the goals arriving in a clinical second-half burst and a stoppage-time finish that underlined the gulf in class — a deflating exit for a side that managed just one point across three matches. Having finished fourth in Group A and crept through as one of the best third-placed teams, they were nonetheless unable to sustain that precarious foothold, and Mexico's composed, purposeful display exposed their limitations emphatically. The Czechs depart at the earliest opportunity, their tournament leaving little to build on.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo led England for the better part of an hour in the Round of 32, Cipenga's seventh-minute opener giving them every reason to believe before Harry Kane dismantled the dream with two clinical finishes in the final quarter-hour. It was a cruel, late unraveling — the Leopards had defended resolutely and threatened a genuine upset — only for England's captain to remind everyone why tournament football rewards patience and predatory quality. DR Congo exit at the last-32 stage, their campaign ultimately defined by the agony of a lead surrendered too late to recover.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador's exit arrived without much ceremony — a 0-2 defeat to Mexico in the Round of 32 in which they never truly threatened, conceding twice before half-time through Quiñones and a Jiménez strike deep in first-half stoppage time. The pattern was emphatic: Ecuador chasing the game from the half-hour mark, unable to find a response across 90 minutes. They had navigated the group stage only as one of the better third-placed sides, and against a Mexican outfit with genuine tournament ambition on home soil, that fragile foundation gave way entirely.

Egypt.text: Egypt edged through their final group fixture against Iran in nervy fashion, taking a fifth-minute lead through Saber only to be pegged back nine minutes later — a draw that was enough to seal second place in Group G but hardly inspired confidence. They meet Australia in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a tie that looks navigable on paper yet carries genuine risk against a Socceroos side never short of organisation and fight. Survive that, and Argentina could await in the Round of 16 — a vastly sterner examination of Egypt's ambitions.

England.text: England survived a genuine scare against DR Congo, Harry Kane's brace rescuing them with goals in the 75th and 86th minutes after Cipenga had given the Congolese an early lead that held until late. Kane's clinical finishing steadied what had been an uncomfortable afternoon. Mexico await in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 17:00 PT — a serious examination, not a formality, as El Tri carry the confidence of a host nation and the tie has every hallmark of a ferocious contest. Survive that, and Brazil or Norway could loom in the quarterfinal, with France or Spain possible opponents should England reach the final.

France.text: France dismantled Sweden without breaking sweat, Mbappé bookending the first and second halves and Barcola adding a third early after the interval — a 3-0 win that carried the authority of a team not yet near its ceiling. Paraguay await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT, a tie France enter as heavy favourites against a side that will make the game compact and combative but lack the quality to seriously threaten a defence this organised. Should Les Bleus advance, Portugal or Spain could loom in the semifinal — the kind of opposition that would constitute a genuine examination of their title credentials.

Germany.text: Germany's Round of 32 exit is as brutal as the format can produce: Havertz's second-half equaliser cancelled out Enciso's opener, and when neither side could be separated across 90 minutes, Paraguay held their nerve in the shootout, converting four of four while Germany stumbled to three. A group-stage winner undone before the last sixteen, Joachim Löw's side — or whoever currently stewards this generation — departs a tournament they entered with genuine ambitions, beaten not by a superior team across the pitch but by the cold lottery of penalties.

Ghana.text: Ghana finished their group stage on the back foot, falling 2-1 to Croatia in a match that slipped away late — Luckassen's 73rd-minute equaliser briefly promised a draw before Vlasic settled it in the 83rd. They advance to the Round of 32 as a best third-placed team, where Colombia await on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT: a genuine test against a side with considerably more pedigree at this stage, though Ghana's resilience through the group suggests they will not be straightforward opponents. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland could follow — with Argentina potentially looming in the quarterfinal if the Black Stars find their best form.

Haiti.text: Haiti depart the 2026 World Cup without a point to their name, their group stage exit confirmed by a 4-2 defeat to Morocco that was comfortable for the Atlas Lions despite a spirited Haitian showing either side of half-time. Two goals inside the first 43 minutes gave Haiti brief cause for optimism, but Morocco reasserted themselves emphatically in the closing stages, scoring four times in total to send their opponents home. The Caribbeans finish bottom of Group C, undone by a gulf in quality that proved too wide to bridge across three matches.

Iran.text: Iran's group-stage finale against Egypt ended 1-1, a Rezaeian equaliser on 14 minutes cancelling out Saber's early opener — a pattern that defined their entire tournament: always in the contest, never quite through it. Three draws, no wins, and three points were enough to sneak through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the knockout round delivered the reckoning that the group stage had deferred. Iran exit the 2026 World Cup without a victory to their name, their campaign a study in stubborn resilience that ultimately fell short of the cutting edge a deep run demands.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 defeat that was effectively settled inside the opening minutes and then ruthlessly extended across the second half, with Gueye alone scoring twice in a 12-minute spell. Finishing fourth in Group I with three losses, no points, and twelve goals conceded, Iraq's tournament was over at the earliest stage — a chastening introduction to a World Cup where the gulf in class between the continent's elite and the tournament's newcomers was laid painfully bare.

Ivory Coast.text: Ivory Coast gave Norway a genuine contest in the Round of 32, pulling level through Diallo on 74 minutes after falling behind to Nusa's first-half opener — only for Haaland to settle it with four minutes remaining, a late hammer blow that extinguished the comeback. The Elephants depart a tournament they had navigated with enough promise to suggest they belonged, yet ultimately could not withstand Norway's cutting edge when it mattered most.

Japan.text: Japan held their own against Brazil for the better part of an hour, Sano's 29th-minute opener giving them genuine belief before Casemiro levelled midway through the second half. It was Martinelli's stoppage-time winner that delivered the cruellest of exits — a Samurai Blue side that had navigated Group F without defeat ultimately undone in the final seconds of a Round of 32 tie they had come agonisingly close to taking further.

Jordan.text: Jordan's group stage ended with a 3-1 defeat to Argentina — a result that followed the script almost from the first whistle, with the South Americans establishing a two-goal cushion inside half an hour before Messi put the contest beyond doubt late on. Al-Tamari's consolation just after the hour offered a brief flicker, but Jordan were outclassed. Finishing fourth in Group J with three defeats and no points, they nevertheless advanced as one of the tournament's best third-placed sides before their World Cup came to a close.

Mexico.text: Mexico arrive at the Round of 16 in the form of a team that has yet to be troubled, a controlled 2-0 dismissal of Ecuador — goals either side of half-time sealing it with something to spare — completing a flawless group stage. England await on Sun Jul 5 17:00 PT, and this is the tie's central tension: Mexico carry genuine momentum and home-continent assurance, yet England's stature makes this a genuine examination rather than a foregone conclusion. Survive that, and Brazil or Norway could stand between El Tri and a semifinal, with the likes of Argentina and Colombia lurking as potential opponents deeper still.

Morocco.text: Morocco survived the most dramatic fashion against the Netherlands, Diop's 90th-minute equaliser forcing penalties before the Atlas Lions edged through 3-2 in the shootout — a nerve-shredding passage that underlined both their resilience and their capacity to conjure moments of decisive quality. Canada await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT, a tie Morocco enter as clear favourites against a side still finding its footing on the world stage, though the Canadians' energy and pace on the break will demand respect. Should they advance, France or Paraguay loom in the quarterfinal, with the prospect of Spain or Portugal casting a long shadow over the semifinal draw.

Netherlands.text: Netherlands dominated their group with seven points and a healthy goal difference, only to suffer one of the tournament's crueller exits — Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike cancelled out by a Moroccan equaliser deep in stoppage time, before the Dutch lost the subsequent shootout 2-3. It was the worst kind of departure: a lead surrendered at the death, then the lottery of penalties delivering the final blow. A side that had looked like genuine contenders through the group stage goes home from the Round of 32, undone in the space of twenty minutes.

New Zealand.text: Belgium administered a thorough lesson in the final group game, with Trossard's brace and further goals from De Bruyne and Lukaku turning what might have been a contest into a rout — New Zealand's consolation arriving far too late, in the 84th minute, to suggest any real foothold. The All Whites finished fourth in Group G with a single point and a goals difference of minus ten, their tournament record a frank reflection of the gulf between them and the competition's established forces. They depart a World Cup that offered little comfort beyond the experience itself.

Norway.text: Norway edged past Ivory Coast in a Round of 32 tie that stayed open far longer than the Scandinavians would have wanted — Nusa's first-half opener was cancelled out by Diallo with sixteen minutes left before Haaland settled nerves with a late winner. The reward is a Round of 16 date with Brazil on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, and few neutrals will give Norway much of a chance: this is a considerable step up in class, and Seleção arrive as heavy favourites against a side that needed an 86th-minute goal to see off African opposition. Should they produce the upset of the tournament, England or Mexico await in the quarterfinal — and the draw opens further from there, with Argentina and France lurking as possibilities deep in the bracket.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was as comprehensive as the record suggests — three defeats, no goals scored, and a closing loss to England that was settled efficiently in a five-minute spell midway through the second half, Bellingham and Kane putting the game to bed without drama or late anxiety. They finish bottom of Group L with nothing to show from their three outings, and whatever hope they carried into the tournament evaporated long before the knockout rounds began. A familiar story for Central American football at this level: the gap between qualifying and competing proved too wide to bridge.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay's elimination of Germany is the tournament's great upset so far — Enciso's first-half strike answered by Havertz just after the break, then a 4-3 shootout triumph that few outside Asunción dared to dream. France await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT, a fixture that looks, on paper, like a steep hill: Les Bleus carry the weight of genuine title expectation, and Paraguay, third-placed in Group D, are firmly the underdogs — though a side that has just beaten Germany on penalties commands respect. Should they find a way through, Canada or Morocco loom in the quarterfinal, with Portugal or Spain among the possible tests in the semifinal beyond that.

Portugal.text: Portugal closed out the group stage with a goalless draw against Colombia — a contained, uneventful affair that secured second place in Group K without any particular statement of intent. They meet Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a genuinely testing assignment against a side with the tournament pedigree and tactical discipline to trouble anyone; Portugal enter as the narrow favorites, but this is no formality. Survive that, and Spain could await in the Round of 16, with the possibility of Belgium or the United States lurking at the quarterfinal stage.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina in which they trailed by two goals before half-time, Al-Haydos pulling one back on the stroke of the interval before Mahmic's 80th-minute strike killed any lingering hope of a comeback. The hosts finished bottom of Group B with a single point and a goal difference of minus eight, their tournament a sobering exercise in the gap between ambition and execution at this level.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde — a stalemate that was as flat as the scoreline suggests, neither side able to manufacture a breakthrough across ninety minutes. That result, combined with a goal difference of minus five across three matches, proved just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed teams, but the Green Falcons were eliminated in the Round of 32, their tournament ending as quietly as it had begun.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament before it had truly begun, a 3-0 defeat that was every bit as one-sided as the scoreline suggests. Júnior struck twice in the first half — the second arriving deep in added time — before Cunha added a third shortly after the interval, leaving Scotland with nothing to build on. They depart having advanced from the group stage as one of the best third-placed sides, a modest distinction that flatters a campaign yielding just one win and four goals conceded. The 2026 World Cup ends for Scotland at the Round of 32.

Senegal.text: Senegal led Belgium twice and carried that advantage deep into extra time, only for Romelu Lukaku to level in the 86th minute and Youri Tielemans to strike twice in three agonising minutes — the second a penalty in stoppage time of extra time — to seal a 3-2 defeat that was as cruel as knockout football gets. The Lions of Teranga had done enough to advance from Group I as one of the best third-placed teams, yet Belgium proved a step too far in the Round of 32. Their tournament ends there: a gutting exit for a side that gave everything and were ultimately undone by a three-minute collapse they could not have scripted in a nightmare.

South Africa.text: South Africa's World Cup ended in the cruelest fashion, a 90th-minute goal from Eustáquio giving Canada a 1-0 win that the balance of the match may not have fully demanded. Bafana Bafana had navigated the group stage with enough grit to finish second in Group A, but a tournament played on home soil — or at least in their confederate hemisphere — ultimately closed in the dying seconds of a Round of 32 tie they very nearly survived.

South Korea.text: South Korea close their World Cup 2026 account with a flat 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's goal just past the hour mark enough to settle a match the Taeguk Warriors never truly threatened to turn. They had squeezed through the group phase as one of the best third-placed sides, but that slender lifeline ran out in the round of 32 — eliminated without a goal scored in their final two outings, their tournament ending as quietly as it had begun.

Spain.text: Spain closed out Group H with a controlled, professional 1-0 victory over Uruguay, Baena's first-half strike doing just enough to preserve a clean sheet and confirm top spot with an unbeaten, goalless-against record. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT — a manageable assignment on paper, and Spain's group-stage composure gives little reason to expect otherwise. Should they advance through the last 16, where Croatia or Portugal could provide a sterner examination of their credentials, a quarterfinal against Belgium or the host United States looms as a potentially defining moment on the road to Sun Jul 19 12:00 PT.

Sweden.text: Sweden's tournament ends with a 0-3 defeat to France in the Round of 32 — a verdict that was effectively delivered at half-time, Mbappé's first strike just before the break puncturing whatever hope the Swedes had carried into the tie, and a Barcola goal eight minutes into the second half rendering the contest settled long before his second. They had advanced from Group F as one of the better third-placed sides, a narrow path that always pointed toward exactly this kind of reckoning: a French side operating at a different altitude. The margin was honest.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland finished their group campaign with a composed 2-1 win over Canada, scoring twice in the opening eleven minutes of the second half through Vargas and Manzambi before absorbing a late consolation — controlled, professional, and exactly the kind of performance that earns top spot. They face Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie Switzerland enter as clear favourites; the Swiss are too technically accomplished for this to be anything other than a routine assignment, provided they bring the same clinical efficiency. Survive that, and a quarterfinal collision with Argentina could await — the one fixture that would sharply raise the stakes.

Tunisia.text: Tunisia's group stage exit was written early: two Dutch goals inside seven minutes buried the Carthage Eagles before the match had found its shape, and though Mastouri pulled one back after the break, Hecke's strike shortly after the hour restored the three-goal cushion and sealed a 3-1 defeat. Tunisia finished bottom of Group F without a point, their record of three defeats and a goal difference of minus ten telling the story of a tournament in which they were consistently outclassed — a dispiriting campaign ended against one of the competition's heavyweights.

Turkey.text: Turkey's tournament ends in the cruelest of fashions — a 3-2 win over the United States that arrived too late to matter. They trailed inside three minutes, fought back to lead through Güler and Yilmaz, conceded again early in the second half, and only sealed it through Ayhan's injury-time strike. The drama was real, the effort undeniable, but a fourth-place group finish — even as one of the better third-placed sides — proved an insufficient foundation, and Turkey's World Cup 2026 is over at the group stage.

USA.text: The USA closed the group stage with a bruising 3-2 defeat to Turkey — Trusty's third-minute opener gave early hope, but Güler and Yilmaz put the Turks firmly in control before the break, and an agonizing stoppage-time goal deep into added time confirmed the loss, even if first place was already secured. Bosnia & Herzegovina arrive first on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT in the Round of 32, a winnable assignment against a side the Americans, as group winners and hosts, will be expected to dispatch. Belgium then loom in the Round of 16 on Mon Jul 6 17:00 PT — a stiffer examination against a nation with genuine pedigree, though one whose golden generation has faded, making this a genuine contest rather than a foregone conclusion. Survive that, and the quarterfinal draw could produce Portugal or Spain — either would represent the sternest test yet of how far this American side can genuinely go.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Álex Baena's first-half strike the solitary difference in a contest that offered the Uruguayans little joy in the final third. Despite advancing as one of the best third-placed sides, their tournament came to an abrupt halt at the same juncture — two draws and a loss across Group H, with a negative goal difference, telling a story of a side that never quite found its footing at this World Cup.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan's group stage ended with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo, a result that traced a familiar arc: an encouraging early lead through Shomurodov, then a second-half capitulation as Wissa and Mayele dismantled them after the hour mark, Wissa adding a third in stoppage time to put a harsh gloss on the scoreline. The Central Asians finished bottom of Group K without a point, conceding eleven goals across three matches, and no late reprieve arrived. Their debut on the expanded World Cup stage was brief and bruising, a tournament that exposed the gulf between ambition and readiness at the highest level.
